projects.name: RELIABLEFLOW
projects.summary: A FastAPI/PostgreSQL platform for background jobs and signed webhook delivery: API-key auth, worker retries, dead-letter replay, cron schedules, metrics, Docker Compose, CI, and a React admin dashboard.
details: PostgreSQL-backed worker loop claims pending jobs with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, records attempts, retries failures with exponential backoff, and promotes exhausted jobs to dead letter.

Webhook layer stores signed delivery records, HMAC-verifies outbound test events, exposes delivery/attempt inspection, and documents the current limitation that no background webhook dispatcher poller is wired.
Portfolio polish includes Docker Compose services, live migration checks in CI, frontend smoke tests, screenshots, architecture docs, demo docs, and explicit known limitations.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: ReliableFlow is a background-job and webhook-delivery platform. API clients submit jobs with idempotency keys; workers claim and process queued work; failed jobs retry with backoff and eventually move to a dead-letter queue; schedules create future jobs; operators inspect queue health, webhook deliveries, and replay paths through a React dashboard.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: FastAPI exposes the API surface; PostgreSQL is the queue of record; async SQLAlchemy drives transactions; Alembic manages schema changes; Redis is health-checked but not used as the queue backend. The worker uses PostgreSQL row-level locking for job claiming, records JobAttempt rows, and calls create_deliveries_for_job on success to store pending WebhookDelivery rows. The admin dashboard is a Vite/React SPA with mock mode plus API-key-backed live mode.

sections.label: 03 — ENGINEERING FOCUS
sections.body: The project is strongest as a reliability and systems-design story: transaction boundaries, idempotent job creation, retry policy, dead-letter replay, audit events, migration discipline, and honest documentation of what is proven versus what remains mocked or deferred.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Finished portfolio project. Local demo path, screenshots, tests, CI, architecture docs, and resume bullets are included. Known limits are explicit: no live multi-process SKIP LOCKED proof, no background webhook dispatcher poller, no exactly-once delivery guarantee, and no production deployment.

projects.name: AI ASSISTANT ACROSS WEBSITES
projects.summary: A Chrome extension + local Node backend that injects an AI assistant into any webpage — summarize, extract, save notes, search them later by vector similarity.
details: Manifest V3 extension with service-worker background, content-script panel, and a separate options page.
Local Node backend persists notes to JSON; saves can mirror to any folder on disk via File System Access API or the backend's NOTES_DIR.

Extension is multi-provider (Gemini/OpenAI/Anthropic with built-in fallback); backend is Gemini-only. Memory search combines keyword match + vector similarity over embeddings, with hashed-embedding fallback when no API key is set.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Open a panel on any page and ask it to summarize, rewrite, or extract structured info from what you're reading. Save the output as a note; later, search your notes by keyword + vector similarity. The notes can also be mirrored to a folder on disk so they live outside the browser.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: MV3 service worker brokers messages between the content-script panel and the local Node backend. The extension can call Gemini, OpenAI, or Anthropic directly in built-in mode (deterministic local fallback if no key); the Node backend is Gemini-only. For the extract action, only the Gemini path uses Google's native responseSchema — OpenAI and Anthropic use prompt-based schema instructions. Embeddings stored as NDJSON. Folder mirror on the extension side uses the File System Access API; the folder handle persists in IndexedDB across Chrome restarts (one re-grant per session).

sections.label: 03 — WHAT I LEARNED
sections.body: Content scripts can't call chrome.runtime.openOptionsPage directly — that one's only available to the extension surface, so the click silently no-ops. Had to route through a background-worker message. Generally the MV3 surface is full of these little asymmetries; the docs are right but easy to skim past.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Personal tool, v0.1.0, no real users. The May 2026 update added the on-disk folder mirror and refreshed the model lists to current Gemini/Claude/GPT names. No auth, no multi-user, no Web Store submission — by design.

projects.name: AIHL — SECURE SERVICE BOOKING
projects.summary: Cross-platform service-booking app with three roles (customer / provider / admin). Laravel API + RN/Expo client; auth, roles, listings, bookings with slot checks, Stripe-ready payments, reviews, admin dashboard.
details: Built across 10 staged milestones: foundation → auth → listings/search → booking core → provider workflow → Stripe payments → notifications → reviews → admin dashboard → test+CI hardening.
Stack: Laravel 13 / PHP 8.3 with Sanctum and the official stripe-php SDK; RN 0.81 / Expo SDK 54 with expo-secure-store for token storage; SQLite by default, MySQL via env.

Payments live behind a PAYMENT_DRIVER switch — defaults to a 'fake' driver, with PAYMENT_DRIVER=stripe enabling real Stripe calls. Reviews gated to completed bookings server-side; admin reporting + flagged-issue handling implemented.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: A three-sided booking platform. Customers browse service categories, search providers, check availability, book, reschedule, cancel. Providers manage requests, block their calendar, complete jobs. Admins run a dashboard with reporting and flag handling.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Laravel 13 API with Sanctum for token auth and role-based controllers; SQLite by default with a MySQL .env profile; Stripe SDK behind a PAYMENT_DRIVER switch (fake by default, stripe when configured) so the integration is decoupled from the booking core. Mobile is RN 0.81 / Expo SDK 54 with expo-secure-store for credentials. The repo is structured so each of the 10 build stages is a discrete, reviewable slice — useful for showing the development arc, not just the end state.

sections.label: 03 — DELIBERATE LIMITS
sections.body: Payments default to a 'fake' driver locally; switching to live Stripe is a single env flip but has never been used in anger — no real charges have happened. Reviews can only be left after a booking transitions to completed — enforced server-side, not just hidden in the UI. CI workflow is included; remote deploy was intentionally not performed (this is a portfolio piece, not an operating service).

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: All 10 build stages complete with CI workflow. No production deployment. Solo build.

projects.name: ERROR MONITORING SYSTEM
stack: AZURE APP SERVICE + STATIC WEB APPS
projects.summary: A simplified Sentry/Datadog-style monitoring platform — ingests application error logs, runs alert rules over rolling windows, and serves a dashboard.
details: ASP.NET Core 10 Web API with EF Core 9; clean three-project layout (Core / Infrastructure / API).
Background IHostedService workers run alert checks and log retention; alerts fire when severity-bucketed counts cross a configured threshold within a rolling time window.
JWT bearer auth + BCrypt-hashed passwords; per-application ingestion API keys; xUnit + Moq + FluentAssertions for unit + integration tests.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Apps post error logs (severity, message, stack trace, source app) to an ingestion endpoint authenticated by a per-app API key. The platform stores them, evaluates configurable alert rules over a rolling time window, and surfaces everything in an Angular 21 dashboard with severity-bucketed views and acknowledgement flow.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Layered .NET solution: Core (domain/DTOs/interfaces), Infrastructure (EF Core + repositories + services), API (controllers + background services). Composite index on (Timestamp, Severity) covers the dashboard's most common filter — a single B-tree seek instead of a bitmap AND between two single-column indexes. Background services tick periodically: one evaluates alert rules, the other prunes old logs.

sections.label: 03 — DESIGN CHOICE: COMPOSITE INDEX
sections.body: The README explicitly walks through why (Timestamp, Severity) beats two single-column indexes here: the dashboard's hot query filters on both columns and orders by timestamp, so a single composite seek satisfies the query plan without a sort step. A small thing to write about, but it's the kind of decision that makes monitoring-platform queries fast enough to feel real-time.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build. Runs against SQLite locally and Azure SQL in the prod profile; deploy targets are Azure App Service + Static Web Apps. No live deployment.

projects.name: CRYPTO MARKET DATA PIPELINE
projects.summary: An ETL pipeline that pulls crypto market snapshots from CoinGecko, lands raw JSON in a partitioned data-lake layout, validates with a rules engine, and loads structured rows into a SQLite warehouse. CLI + tiny web/HTTP API.
details: Two run modes: `--mode once` for a single ingestion + load cycle, `--mode daemon --interval 300` for continuous polling.

Data-lake layout: raw JSON lands at `data_lake/raw/coin_gecko/date=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH/markets_*.json` (Hive-style partitioning), then transforms into `dim/fact` tables in SQLite — including a `fact_price_series` table fanned out from CoinGecko's 7-day sparkline.

Rules-engine validation runs before warehouse load: row-level (required fields, numeric ranges, symbol regex, ISO timestamps) + batch-level (duplicate keys). Invalid rows rejected; a validation report lands alongside the raw payload.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Polls CoinGecko for the top N coins (default 50), captures snapshot fields and the 7-day sparkline, lands the raw JSON in a partitioned lake, validates it, then loads the clean rows into a SQLite warehouse. A small Flask web app sits on top exposing /api/run-once, /api/latest, and /api/series/<symbol>.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The pipeline is split into discrete modules — extractor (HTTP via requests), transformer (sparkline fan-out + dataset shaping), validation (rules engine), raw_store (lake writer), warehouse (sqlite3 schema + loader). main.py is the CLI; app.py exposes the same backend over HTTP via Flask. SQL analytics queries live alongside in crypto_pipeline/sql/.

sections.label: 03 — WHY THE RAW-LAKE LAYER
sections.body: I could have written transformed rows straight to SQLite. Keeping the raw JSON landing zone means the pipeline is replayable — if the schema changes or a transform has a bug, I can reload from raw without re-hitting CoinGecko's rate limit. The validation report writes to the lake too, so failures don't disappear.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build. Pytest suite covers validation + pipeline integration. Single-machine SQLite warehouse — the architecture would translate to Postgres/Snowflake but the project's not wired for it.

projects.name: AI CANDIDATE DASHBOARD
projects.summary: A hiring-workflow backend that ingests resumes, extracts structured fields with a Celery worker, and exposes recruiter-facing candidate review APIs. The frontend exists as a static prototype while the React/Vite rebuild is still in progress.
details: Async resume processing: PDF/text upload triggers a Celery job that extracts skills, years of experience, education, summary, and a match score against a job description.

Reviewer workflow: search, status/tag/score filtering, pagination, candidate detail with evidence snippets, notes, tags, and a six-state lifecycle (Applied → Screened → Interview → Offer → Hired, with Rejected as a terminal path).
Reliability/ops: Pydantic schema validation on extraction output, Celery retries with backoff, Redis-backed queueing, structured JSON logs, persisted JobFailure model tracking retry counts.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Upload a resume (PDF/text) + a job description. A background worker extracts structured candidate fields — skills, years of experience, education, summary, match score — and surfaces them in a paginated dashboard. Recruiters can filter by status/tag/score, drill into evidence snippets, leave notes, and move candidates through a lifecycle state machine.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Django/DRF API + a Celery worker queue backed by Redis. The extractor returns a payload validated by Pydantic before it hits Postgres. Failed jobs are persisted with their retry count so they're recoverable, not silent. The backend pipeline is implemented; the modern frontend wiring is still being rebuilt.

sections.label: 03 — WHY IT'S BUILT THIS WAY
sections.body: Resume parsing is the kind of work where the failure modes matter as much as the happy path — you need retries because LLMs flake, schema validation because LLMs hallucinate fields, and persisted failure records because silent dropped jobs in a recruiter tool would be much worse than a visible error. Most of the engineering went into those seams.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo project. Backend pipeline and resume-processing flow are implemented; frontend/package wiring is in progress. No production deploy or real users — built as a portfolio piece showing the async-extract pattern.

projects.name: AI INBOX COPILOT
projects.summary: A pragmatic Superhuman/Grammarly-adjacent inbox assistant — Gmail OAuth sync, LLM-backed thread summaries, draft reply generation with tone controls, and follow-up reminders.
details: Gmail OAuth sign-in via Auth.js v5; sync merges INBOX + SENT thread IDs so outbound replies land in the local thread model.

Thread summarization, draft generation with tone/clarifying-question modes, and natural-language draft revision — backed by a Claude → Gemini → heuristic-template provider chain, so a missing key in one provider falls through gracefully.

OAuth tokens encrypted at rest in `.data/inbox.json` with AES-256-GCM when ENCRYPTION_KEY is set; backend webhook routes for Gmail watch + Microsoft Graph subscriptions exist but aren't wired into the current UI.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Connect a Gmail account, sync threads, and ask the copilot to summarize a thread, draft a reply (with tone controls), or revise a draft from a natural-language edit instruction. Mark threads as 'waiting on' to get a follow-up reminder. Search is keyword-only today; the /api/search route is the seam for adding embeddings later.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Next 15 App Router + Auth.js v5 (beta) for OAuth. Local JSON persistence in .data/inbox.json — no real DB at runtime, even though Drizzle's schema/client/migrations are wired in. LLM calls try Claude first, then fall back to Gemini, then to heuristic templates. OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM if ENCRYPTION_KEY is set, plaintext otherwise. Backend Gmail-watch and Microsoft-Graph webhook routes exist, but only the Gmail UI flow is wired end-to-end.

sections.label: 03 — DELIBERATE LIMITS
sections.body: Local JSON instead of Postgres because the storage shape was still moving and migrations would have been overhead. Multi-provider LLM with a cascade so the app degrades gracefully when an API key isn't set. Microsoft Graph code is left in but UI-disabled — the connector works at the adapter layer, just not promoted to a user-facing flow.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: MVP, no production deploy. Built as a portfolio piece — the docs/ folder includes the locked architecture decisions (D1–D8) and a remediation plan that documents what would need to change to take it past MVP.

projects.name: AI READING ASSISTANT
projects.summary: A local-first Electron reading workspace for PDF study: import documents, extract/search text, OCR scanned pages, annotate sections, assemble retrieval-grounded study context, generate study outputs, review saved answers, and export PDFs.
details: PDF workflow: app-owned library storage, SHA-256 dedupe, last-page restore, spread/single-page reader modes, embedded-text extraction, SQLite FTS5 search, OCR fallback, and local thumbnails.

Study workflow: manual sections, highlights, page notes, retrieval context with citations, provider settings for OpenAI/Anthropic/Gemini, multi-step generation, saved outputs, review queue, pinned snippets, progress aggregates, and PDF export.
Packaging hardening: macOS/Windows unpacked targets, resource checks, CSP/security hardening, packaged smoke checks, and 395 passing tests documented in the M11 capstone report.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: AI Reading Assistant is a desktop app for active reading. Import a local PDF, read with saved progress, extract text or OCR scanned pages, search across the library, mark sections and annotations, ask the assistant to build source-grounded study material, save useful outputs, and export a polished study guide.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Electron owns the filesystem, SQLite database, migrations, provider keys, and secure IPC. The React renderer stays inside typed app channels. pdfjs-dist renders and extracts PDFs; tesseract.js handles OCR; SQLite FTS5 powers search; better-sqlite3 stores documents, chunks, annotations, saved study outputs, review items, and export jobs. Provider adapters stream generation through explicit OpenAI, Anthropic, and Gemini settings.

sections.label: 03 — DESIGN FOCUS
sections.body: The product is intentionally not just an AI summary button. The reading cycle is built around recall, diagnosis, repair, notes from the learner's own understanding, and later review. That shaped the source-grounded retrieval layer, saved study workspace, review queue, and export flow.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Finished local desktop portfolio build. M0-M11 are accepted with verifier logs; signing/notarization, macOS x64 launch smoke, and Windows launch smoke remain platform-bound follow-ups.

projects.name: CLIENTFLOW
projects.summary: A multi-tenant client operations portal: org-scoped auth, role-based tickets, comments, SLA tracking, dashboards, weekly reports, simulated notifications, attachment metadata, Docker Compose, and CI.
details: Role model covers PLATFORM_ADMIN, COMPANY_MANAGER, STAFF, and CLIENT_USER with organization isolation across tickets, rosters, dashboards, comments, notifications, reports, and attachments.

Ticket workflow includes assignment, status transitions through a state machine, client-visible versus internal comments, SLA due-soon/overdue/paused/resolved tracking, and dashboard/workload/activity APIs.
Capstone 9 adds testing, CI, Docker Compose polish, seeded demo accounts, weekly Markdown report generation, simulated notification delivery, and attachment metadata without binary file storage.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: ClientFlow is an internal support portal for client-facing teams. Users sign in as platform admin, company manager, staff, or client; create and triage tickets; add client-visible or internal comments; watch SLA pressure; generate weekly reports; inspect notification history; and record attachment metadata on ticket detail pages.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Spring Boot exposes JWT-protected APIs backed by PostgreSQL and Flyway migrations. The React/Vite frontend uses protected routes, role-gated navigation, dashboard cards, workload tables, ticket filters, and report/notification views. Docker Compose brings up the backend, frontend, and database with seeded demo accounts for two organizations.

sections.label: 03 — ENGINEERING FOCUS
sections.body: The strongest part is the permissions and lifecycle discipline: every feature has to respect organization boundaries and role differences. SLA status, report generation, comments, notification visibility, and attachment metadata all exercise that boundary instead of treating the app as generic CRUD.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Capstone 9 is implemented. Public self-registration, refresh tokens, real email delivery, binary file storage, audit logs, cloud deployment, and deeper production hardening are documented as future work.

projects.name: COMMERCEOPS
projects.summary: A Next.js commerce operations platform with a public storefront and admin dashboard: products, cart, coupons, Stripe test checkout, webhook-driven orders, inventory, CMS/blog, SEO drafts, CSV import/export, and operational alerts.
details: Checkout re-prices the cart from the database, stores money as integer cents, applies exact coupon discounts, and creates orders from Stripe webhooks rather than trusting the success redirect.
Inventory decrement uses a guarded Prisma update with quantity checks so concurrent checkout cannot oversell; webhook processing is raw-body signature verified and idempotent.
Admin surface covers product CRUD/archive, order fulfillment, inventory events, low-stock and abandoned-cart alerts, CSV import/export, CMS pages/blog posts, and deterministic SEO draft generation.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: CommerceOps has two sides: a customer storefront for browsing products, managing a cart, applying coupons, and checking out through Stripe test mode; and an admin operations dashboard for catalog, inventory, orders, alerts, CMS content, CSV import/export, and SEO drafts.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Next.js route handlers own the storefront and admin APIs. Prisma maps the PostgreSQL schema. Guest carts use an HTTP-only token cookie, checkout creates a Stripe session from database-priced cart contents, and the Stripe webhook creates the order transaction. Admin auth is a bcrypt-backed, HMAC-signed, database-revocable session cookie with same-origin checks on mutations.

sections.label: 03 — ENGINEERING FOCUS
sections.body: The useful proof is not that it has a cart. It is that money, inventory, and webhooks are treated as correctness problems: database re-pricing, integer cents, idempotent webhook events, unique session IDs, and guarded inventory decrement all have source evidence and automated tests.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Finished portfolio build with CI, Docker Compose, demo data, screenshots, and a demo script. Product variants, real shipping/tax, real refunds, Shopify import, analytics, and richer CMS permissions are future improvements.

projects.name: CLINICAL IMAGING PIPELINE & QA DASHBOARD
projects.summary: A reproducible biomedical image-analysis pipeline prototype for PneumoniaMNIST: ingest, validate, train, evaluate, track lineage in SQLite, and inspect dataset/model QA through a Streamlit dashboard.
details: Pipeline stages cover database init, MedMNIST ingest, SQL-only validation checks, deterministic PyTorch baseline training, scikit-learn evaluation, and sidecar artifacts for metrics, ROC/PR curves, and confusion matrices.
SQLite lineage ties every model artifact, prediction, and QA check back to a pipeline run so the dashboard can explain where each result came from.

Streamlit/Plotly dashboard includes split and label distributions, prediction confidence histograms, confusion matrix heatmap, ROC/PR curves, run duration charts, image QC, Grad-CAM, saliency, and graceful empty-state guidance.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: The project takes the standardized PneumoniaMNIST benchmark from ingest through validation, model training, evaluation, and QA review. The dashboard lets a reviewer inspect dataset shape, validation checks, image samples, predictions, model metrics, and pipeline history from the same SQLite-backed surface.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Python CLIs and Makefile targets run the stages: initialize schema, ingest the dataset, validate records, train a small BaselineCNN, evaluate the saved model, and launch Streamlit. Training and evaluation write model artifacts plus metrics sidecars; the dashboard reads lineage and prediction tables without needing to rerun training.

sections.label: 03 — SAFETY BOUNDARY
sections.body: The repo is explicit that this is a research and educational prototype only. PneumoniaMNIST is a downsampled benchmark, not raw clinical DICOM; the app is not a diagnostic system, not a medical device, and not suitable for medical decisions.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: M0-M4 are implemented with offline tests and documentation. Clinical validation, regulatory clearance, fairness/calibration analysis, larger models, and real radiology workflow support are out of scope.

projects.name: DISTRIBUTED JOB QUEUE + WEBHOOK DELIVERY
projects.summary: A PostgreSQL-backed job queue backend with FastAPI APIs, worker leases, retry scheduling, dead-letter handling, webhook delivery, worker heartbeats, queue stats, and metrics endpoints.
details: Public job APIs create, list, cancel, retry, and inspect queued work; internal worker APIs claim jobs, renew leases, complete work, fail jobs, and register heartbeats.
Workers claim runnable pending jobs or expired running jobs with PostgreSQL locking, priority order, lease tokens, visible-until deadlines, and deterministic retry/backoff rules.
Webhook delivery treats 2xx as success, selected 4xx as permanent failure, 408/429/5xx/timeouts as retryable failures, and invalid URLs as permanent failures.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: API callers create jobs into named queues. Worker processes claim runnable jobs, execute supported handlers such as sleep_test, mock_email, or deliver_webhook, renew leases for long work, then complete, retry later, cancel, or move failed work to a dead-letter queue.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: FastAPI exposes public job endpoints plus internal worker endpoints guarded by an internal token. PostgreSQL stores the queue state; async SQLAlchemy wraps the transactions; workers use row locks and lease tokens so expired running jobs can be reclaimed without stale workers completing jobs they no longer own.

sections.label: 03 — DESIGN CHOICE: LEASES
sections.body: A worker can disappear mid-job, so ownership is time-bound. Complete, fail, and renew operations must present the matching unexpired lease token, which keeps stale workers from finishing jobs they no longer own and makes retries deterministic.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Finished local-first v1. No distributed tracing, no tenant isolation, no external scheduler beyond workers polling run_at, and no exactly-once guarantee for external side effects. ReliableFlow is the newer, more polished portfolio follow-up.

projects.name: AIHL — B2B PLATFORM
projects.summary: A B2B financial-services platform: Laravel 13 REST API + Livewire admin portal + a React Native mobile client. Auth, accounts, transactions, statement PDFs, scheduled reports, third-party sync with HMAC-verified webhooks.
details: Backend serves two audiences: mobile clients via versioned JSON API (/api/v1/...) authenticated with JWT, and internal staff via a Livewire admin panel using Laravel session auth.
Mobile (RN 0.76 / Expo SDK 52) handles JWT storage in react-native-keychain, silent token refresh via Axios interceptors, paginated transaction lists, and asynchronous PDF statement download.

Backend services include incremental third-party provider sync every 15 min, HMAC-SHA256 webhook receiver with idempotency-key replay protection, queue-backed PDF/CSV generation, and scheduled daily/monthly reports.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Customer-facing mobile app: log in, see your account balance and transactions, request a PDF statement for any date range, manage notifications and profile. Internal admin portal at /admin: manage users and accounts, watch live third-party sync logs, generate daily transaction CSVs and monthly account-summary CSVs.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Laravel 13 / PHP 8.3 backend with Spatie roles, JWT for the API and session auth for the admin. Livewire 3 powers the admin UI with realtime updates. Long-running work — PDF statements, CSV reports, notifications — runs through Laravel queues with the database driver. Webhooks verify HMAC-SHA256 signatures and dedupe by idempotency key. Mobile is RN 0.76 / Expo 52 with TanStack Query for server state, Zustand for client state.

sections.label: 03 — WHY TWO AUTH FLOWS
sections.body: Mobile clients need stateless tokens (JWT) so they can refresh silently and survive offline; admin staff use a single session in a browser, so Laravel's session guard is the right tool. Mixing them in one auth layer would have meant a worse fit for both. The Spatie permission package handles roles cleanly across both.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build. Backend uses SQLite in dev, PHPUnit 12 for tests. No remote deploy.

projects.name: CLIPSYNC
projects.summary: A cross-device clipboard-sync daemon written in Go. Peers discover each other on the local network via mDNS, pair with a one-time secret, and exchange clipboard updates over QUIC/TLS using length-prefixed JSON envelopes shaped for a future proto migration.
details: Daemon polls the system clipboard at a configurable interval (default 200 ms) and emits structured JSON log lines on every change.
Cross-platform clipboard: macOS, Linux (X11 + Wayland), Windows. Integration tests with fake clipboards run cross-platform; the real-clipboard test requires a graphical macOS session.

Networking layer is a hand-rolled stack: mDNS discovery, an identity/pairing/roster module, QUIC/TLS transport, and a length-prefixed JSON envelope (Hello / ClipboardUpdate / Ack) kept schema-compatible with the proto3 definitions — not gRPC.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Run the daemon on every device you own, pair them once, and your clipboard follows you across machines. CLI is Cobra-built: `daemon`, `get`/`set`, `ping`, `pair host` / `pair join`, `devices list` / `devices remove`. Configuration lives in TOML at the platform-appropriate config dir.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Internal Go packages: clipboard (polling abstraction per OS), mdns (peer discovery on the local network), pairing (one-time secret exchange), identity + crypto (device keys, signatures), roster (known peers), transport + tls (QUIC/TLS), wire (length-prefixed JSON envelopes kept compatible with proto3 message shapes). Daemon polls clipboard, hashes the content, and broadcasts a ClipboardUpdate envelope to peers when it changes; receivers ack.

sections.label: 03 — WHY NOT gRPC
sections.body: gRPC would have been faster to set up but heavier than the problem deserved. The current codec is a compact length-prefixed JSON envelope, but the message shape tracks the proto3 schema so a protobuf migration would not change the domain model. Keeping the transport thin makes reconnect and pairing behavior explicit.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build. Daemon + CLI work end-to-end on macOS; cross-platform clipboard support is in code, but only the macOS real-clipboard path is exercised by the live integration test.

projects.name: CITYPULSE
projects.summary: A React + Express geospatial dashboard for exploring urban signals on an interactive Mapbox/Deck.gl map. It combines air-quality, weather, and transit-style layers with sidebar controls, charts, backend proxy routes, and WebSocket updates.
details: Client: React 18 + Vite with Redux Toolkit state, Mapbox rendering, Deck.gl layers, sidebar controls, location autocomplete, Recharts summaries, and Jest/React Testing Library coverage.

Server: Express proxy routes for /api/weather, /api/air-quality, and /api/transit, cache middleware, WebSocket live-update channel, and fallback fixtures when the legacy OpenAQ v2 endpoint returns 410 Gone.
Operational shape: workspace scripts for client/server dev, production build for the client, server tests with node:test, Docker Compose files, and GitHub Actions that run client and server tests.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Search for a U.S. city, recenter the map, and inspect air quality, weather stations, and transit-density fixtures as toggleable map layers. The sidebar shows current context and charts so the map is not the only way to read the data.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The React client owns map state and layer toggles in Redux, renders Deck.gl layers on top of Mapbox, and calls the Express API through a configurable VITE_API_BASE_URL. The server proxies OpenWeather and OpenAQ-style requests, serves local transit fixture data, caches responses by URL, and starts a WebSocket server for periodic air-quality updates.

sections.label: 03 — DATA LIMITS
sections.body: Weather requests are proxied through the backend and require an OpenWeather key. Air quality currently falls back to fixture data when the old OpenAQ v2 endpoint returns 410 Gone, and transit is fixture-backed. That makes the UI demo stable while keeping the migration point explicit.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build. Client tests, server tests, and client production build are documented as passing. Local run uses separate client/server dev commands or Docker Compose; no production deployment is recorded.

projects.name: SPARK DATA PIPELINE
projects.summary: A configurable Spark ETL: ingests raw datasets (parquet/csv/json), enforces schema, dedupes, lands a partitioned Parquet warehouse, and writes KPI/aggregation tables to either a Parquet sink or a JDBC database. Bonus: a Streamlit UI for the NYC TLC taxi flow.
details: Generic pipeline (main.py) with a fixed expected schema (event_time, user_id, region, device_type, event_type, metric_value); does schema enforcement, null filtering, normalization, dedup; writes cleaned data partitioned by event_date.
Two result-sink modes: `warehouse` (partitioned Parquet outputs for daily_kpis, device_breakdown, event_type_trends) or `jdbc` (same shape into Postgres or any JDBC-reachable warehouse).
NYC TLC flow (nyc_tlc_trip_workflow.py + Streamlit UI in ui_app.py) downloads and processes NYC taxi parquet files end-to-end.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Point it at a folder of raw parquet/csv/json files and it produces a cleaned partitioned dataset plus three KPI tables (daily_kpis, device_breakdown, event_type_trends). Sink is configurable: write to Parquet for analyst-friendly storage, or push straight to a relational warehouse over JDBC. A separate workflow script handles the NYC Taxi & Limousine Commission datasets specifically.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: PySpark 3.5+ does all the work. CLI surface exposes the knobs that matter at scale: --shuffle-partitions for cluster-size tuning, --repartition for write parallelism / output file count. The Streamlit UI is a thin wrapper over the NYC TLC script — pick taxi type / year / month, it downloads the parquet file from CloudFront and shells out to the workflow.

sections.label: 03 — WHY DUAL SINK
sections.body: Most analytics flows want both: Parquet for cheap storage and ad-hoc query, JDBC for the BI tools that won't speak parquet. Shipping both behind one CLI flag keeps the pipeline reusable across teams that have different downstream tooling.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo project. Not run on a real cluster — designed and tested locally; the --shuffle-partitions and --repartition tuning notes describe how it would scale up.

projects.name: TASK TRACKER (SPRING + ANGULAR)
projects.summary: A multi-user task tracker with full CRUD, per-account list/dashboard scoping, filtering, sorting, pagination, and a dashboard summary endpoint. Spring Boot REST API + Angular 8 SPA.
details: Backend: Spring Boot + Spring Data JPA + Spring Security (HTTP Basic) + Swagger + JUnit. Per-user scoping on list/dashboard endpoints; per-task ownership checks on get/update/delete are a known gap (auth-z hole I'd close before any real deployment).
Schema: users / categories / tasks with explicit indexes on status, priority, due_date, assignee_id, creator_id, category_id — the primary filter/join dimensions for the dashboard and list views.
Frontend: Angular 8 + Bootstrap with login, dashboard, task list (filter/sort/paginate/status-change), and task create/edit forms. Tests via Jasmine + Karma.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Register an account, log in via HTTP Basic, manage your tasks with title/description/status/priority/due-date/creator/assignee/category. Filter by all of those, sort, paginate. The dashboard and list endpoints scope by the logged-in user; per-task endpoints (get/update/delete) currently rely on knowing the right ID — that's the next thing to fix if this were to leave a portfolio context.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Standard Spring Boot stack: JPA repositories, Security with HTTP Basic, JUnit for tests against H2 in-memory; MySQL in regular dev. 19 REST endpoints across /api/auth, /api/tasks, /api/users, /api/categories, and /api/dashboard. Swagger UI at /swagger-ui.html. Frontend is Angular 8 with the routes mapped 1:1 to the API surface.

sections.label: 03 — WHY HTTP BASIC
sections.body: Basic auth is enough for a single-tenant CRUD demo and keeps the focus on the actual data model + querying patterns rather than session/token management. Indexes are explicit on the columns the dashboard queries hit because that's where the API earns its keep — counting tasks by category and priority for an active user has to be a single index seek, not a table scan.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo project. Built as a portfolio piece showing a complete CRUD-+-auth slice end-to-end with reasonable schema design.

projects.name: CUSTOMER ISSUE TRACKER
projects.summary: A full-stack customer support app for issue submission, analyst triage, investigation, and resolution. Spring Boot REST API + Angular 8 SPA with role-aware dashboards and a guarded issue lifecycle.
details: Backend: Spring Boot REST API with HTTP Basic security, role checks for customer/analyst/admin flows, issue CRUD, comments, reference data, dashboard summary, resolution reporting, Swagger/OpenAPI, seeded demo users, and JUnit tests.
Issue lifecycle: OPEN → IN_PROGRESS → INVESTIGATING/RESOLVED → CLOSED with explicit validation and blocked invalid transitions. RESOLVED can reopen to IN_PROGRESS; CLOSED is terminal.
Frontend: Angular 8 + Bootstrap SPA with role-aware routes, auth and role guards, customer submission/detail flow, analyst triage dashboard, admin reporting view, and Jasmine/Karma specs.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Customers submit issues with category, priority, description, and optional attachment. Analysts triage the queue, assign ownership, add comments, and move issues through investigation and resolution. Admins can review dashboard totals and resolution reporting.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Spring Boot exposes /api/issues, /api/reference, /api/dashboard, and /api/auth endpoints over a MySQL-backed JPA model. Spring Security uses HTTP Basic and role-aware service rules. The Angular client routes customers, analysts, and admins to different dashboards through AuthGuard and RoleGuard.

sections.label: 03 — DESIGN CHOICE: STATE MACHINE
sections.body: The issue status is not just a dropdown. The backend owns the allowed transition map, so the UI can be helpful without being trusted. That keeps impossible jumps, like OPEN straight to CLOSED, out of the database even if someone calls the API directly.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Completed portfolio build with seeded credentials for customer, analyst, and admin roles. Backend targets MySQL by default; dependencies were not reinstalled during the latest sandbox verification because registry access is restricted.

projects.name: JAVA BANKING SYSTEM
projects.summary: A multi-user banking backend in Core Java. Clients connect over TCP with a line-based protocol; register / login / open accounts / deposit / withdraw / transfer / history; SQLite persistence; strict layered architecture.
details: Layered architecture (network → service → dao → model + util) with downward-only dependencies as the rule; one small leak — network imports a dao exception type — that an archunit rule would catch.
Custom line-based UTF-8 wire protocol over raw TCP — verbs (REGISTER/LOGIN/OPEN/DEPOSIT/WITHDRAW/TRANSFER/BALANCE/HISTORY) with `|`-separated fields, OK/ERROR responses, max 4096 chars per line.

Account-level locking in the service layer for concurrent safety; sealed Command interface for exhaustive dispatch; Observer pattern wires AccountService → TransactionLogger after every balance-changing op.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Run the server, point one or more console clients at it over TCP, and you have a tiny multi-user bank: register, login, open accounts, deposit, withdraw, transfer between accounts, query balance and transaction history. Persistence is SQLite; schema bootstraps from a bundled schema.sql on first start.

sections.label: 02 — ARCHITECTURE
sections.body: Five packages, downward-only dependencies as the rule: network (TCP server, client, sealed-Command dispatch), service (business rules, account-level locking, money policy), dao (JDBC, PreparedStatement, row mapping), model (immutable records + enums), util (DB singleton, hashing, observer). The architecture.md document spells out the layer rules. There's one self-noted leak — network imports a DataAccessException from dao — exactly the kind of drift you'd catch with an archunit rule if this grew bigger.

sections.label: 03 — WHY HAND-ROLL THE PROTOCOL
sections.body: I could have wrapped this in HTTP/JSON or gRPC and called it a day. The point of the project was to practice the parts those frameworks hide: framing, parsing, command dispatch, error encoding. Sealed-interface Commands give the switch exhaustiveness; the protocol grammar is documented at network/build.md §5 so it's reviewable, not folkloric.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build, intentionally portfolio-scoped: not for production. JUnit 5 + Mockito test suite covers the layers.

projects.name: JVM TUNING BENCHMARK
projects.summary: A URL-shortener service wired for systematic JVM-tuning experiments — multi-profile caching (Caffeine L1 + Redis L2), HikariCP, Flyway migrations, Gatling load, Prometheus + Grafana. Ran the M0–M8 milestones end-to-end: redirect p99 went from 2892 ms → 13 ms.
details: Eight tuning milestones executed (M0 baseline → M1 index+heap+G1 → M2 Caffeine → M3 Redis → M4 pool → M5 GC tuning → M6 virtual threads → M8 generational ZGC). M7 (WebFlux) skipped, M9 (alloc rewrite) deferred. Documented in tuning-journal.md with raw Gatling results.

Three-layer caching: optional Caffeine L1 (10k, 5min TTL), optional Redis L2 (10min TTL), Postgres source of truth. Every /{slug} response sets X-Cache-Source ∈ {L1, L2, DB, MISS} for traceable cache analysis. The original M3 Redis run was a broken-L2 serialization run; after fixing it, L2 still offered no meaningful benefit on the cache-hot 10k working set.

Per-milestone Spring profiles + JVM args in scripts/run-app.sh — switching milestones never requires a rebuild. Observability: Prometheus 5s scrape, three pre-provisioned Grafana dashboards, X-Correlation-Id and X-Response-Time-Ms on every response.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: It's a URL shortener — not because the world needs another one, but because the workload (read-heavy, hot-key skewed, cacheable but not trivially so) is the right shape to expose JVM tuning effects. The service exposes /shorten + /{slug} + /stats/{slug} + /top under /api/v1, with X-API-Key write auth and optional Idempotency-Key on POST. Prometheus + actuator endpoints provide measurement.

sections.label: 02 — THE EXPERIMENT DESIGN
sections.body: M0 is the captured baseline profile: no link-hit index, no Redis L2, 512MB heap, with the app's boot-default L1 behavior documented in the journal. Each subsequent milestone adds one variable: M1 turns on the (slug, time) index via Flyway V2, M2 formalizes Caffeine tuning, M3 adds Redis, M4 tunes Hikari + prep-stmt caching, M5 bumps heap + targets a 50ms G1 pause, M6 swaps to virtual threads, M8 swaps to generational ZGC. Profiles are additive deltas so the comparison stays clean.

sections.label: 03 — INTERESTING RESULTS
sections.body: M1's big win was mostly heap+GC, not the index — the redirect path doesn't actually touch link_hits. The first M3 result exposed a broken-L2 serialization path; after that fix, Redis L2 still did not help because the 10k-slug working set fits in Caffeine and L2 misses only add network RTT. M6 (virtual threads) and M8 (generational ZGC) were no-ops on this workload — cache-hot, low-allocation, requests complete in µs so carrier threads never park and ZGC's pause advantage isn't exercised. All of those are valuable negative results to have in writing.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo. Runs done on Apple M2 / 16 GB / OpenJDK 23 / Postgres 15.6 / Redis 7.2 / Gatling 3.10.5. M7 (WebFlux) skipped as out-of-scope; M9 (allocation-hot-path rewrite) deferred pending JFR analysis. Design doc, journal, and ceiling-sweep results all in repo.

projects.name: MULTI-SOURCE ETL — BRAZILIAN E-COMMERCE
projects.summary: Medallion-architecture (Bronze/Silver/Gold) ETL integrating three sources — Olist Brazilian e-commerce (99k orders / 113k line items), Open-Meteo historical weather (20 cities, ERA5), Frankfurter FX (USD/BRL) — into Parquet Gold outputs and a Postgres warehouse. The 4-page / 27-DAX Power BI dashboard is documented/spec'd, with the .pbix still pending.
details: Bronze preserves raw snapshots — Parquet for the DB-sourced tables, JSON for the API-sourced ones (weather, FX). Silver enforces pandera schema contracts with quarantine routing for invalid rows. Gold materialises 4 Parquet facts and 5 dimensions; 3 facts are warehouse-loaded in Postgres today.

Three independent integration patterns: Kaggle CSV bulk load, Open-Meteo time-series API per city, Frankfurter FX daily series — joined back to fact_sales by order date and shipping city for currency-normalised + weather-aware analysis.

98-test pytest suite running in a Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 matrix on GitHub Actions; one-command Docker Compose pipeline run; 4-page Power BI dashboard with 27 DAX measures is specified in docs, but the .pbix export is still pending.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Builds a unified analytics warehouse that can answer cross-source questions: which Brazilian states drive the most order revenue, does adverse weather correlate with delivery failures, how does USD/BRL volatility move USD-normalised reporting, which sellers ship on time. The warehouse outputs are Postgres + Parquet; the Power BI dashboard is specified but not exported as a finished .pbix yet.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Bronze layer lands raw snapshots of every source — Parquet for DB-sourced tables, JSON for API responses (weather, FX). Silver casts types, dedupes, and runs pandera schema contracts; rejects route to data/silver/quarantine/ rather than failing the whole batch. Gold materialises 4 Parquet facts and 5 dimensions, with 3 facts loaded into the warehouse today. Pipeline runs end-to-end via Make targets / docker compose, with 98 tests covering the transforms.

sections.label: 03 — WHY MEDALLION
sections.body: The schema-contract layer (Silver) is what makes this not-a-toy: invalid rows get isolated to a quarantine path with full provenance, so the pipeline never silently fails or corrupts the analytics layer. Bronze gives me the raw landing zone for replay. Gold's star schema is shaped specifically for the dashboard's question set, not as a generic dump — that's the part most portfolio ETLs skip.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build. Pipeline runs end-to-end; the Power BI dashboard is designed/spec'd, not built/exported as a .pbix yet. Not a deployed service — it's a portfolio piece showing the medallion + multi-source pattern with realistic data volumes.

projects.name: NOTEMIND
projects.summary: An offline-first iOS notes app with a streaming weekly AI summary. Writes hit local SQLite first and sync to Supabase in the background; the summary streams over SSE from a Deno edge function calling Gemini.
details: Offline-first mutations: every write is an optimistic update against op-sqlite; the network call runs in the background; failures retry up to 3 times then persist for replay when connectivity returns. Client-generated UUIDs so the optimistic row and the server row share a single id — no local→remote remapping, no duplicate-create races.

Streaming summary: a Deno edge function calls Gemini with generateContentStream + responseSchema, relays tokens to the client over SSE; the final 'done' frame carries the structured JSON (themes/mood/highlights). Per-user daily token quota enforced in Postgres.

SecureStore with MMKV fallback: on unsigned simulator builds, iOS rejects Keychain writes because the app declares entitlements (Apple Sign In) it can't satisfy. The write is caught and persisted to MMKV so the Supabase session survives reloads in dev — production never hits the fallback.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Take notes offline; tap Summary at the end of the week and get a streaming AI recap of what you wrote — themes, mood, highlights — that links back to source notes. Email-OTP sign-in via Supabase, sessions persist across launches. First word of the streamed summary lands in ~1–3s.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Mobile is Expo SDK 55 in bare workflow on RN 0.83. State is TanStack Query v5 with an MMKV persister + Zustand for auth. Local store is op-sqlite for notes, MMKV for KV. Backend is Supabase: Postgres with RLS policies, Auth, plus a Deno Edge Function that owns the Gemini streaming flow. Observability: Sentry for errors, PostHog for events.

sections.label: 03 — INTERESTING PROBLEMS
sections.body: The two non-obvious decisions are the client-side UUID and the SecureStore fallback. The UUID one removes a whole class of optimistic-update bugs (duplicate-create races) at zero runtime cost. The fallback is the kind of dev-loop fix that rarely makes it into clean architecture diagrams but matters every day you're not on a signed build — write the failure path explicitly, isolate it to dev, document why.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: End-to-end working on the iOS simulator: sign-in, offline note CRUD with background sync, streaming weekly summary. Not yet deployed to a physical device or TestFlight — entitlement work + a real Apple Developer account would be the next step.

projects.name: OIL & GAS PRODUCTION DASHBOARD
projects.summary: Power BI model/report specification in progress for 70+ years of North Dakota Bakken crude oil production. The repo contains ETL structure, DAX files, and report design notes; the .pbix, screenshots, service publish, and final video are still pending.
details: Five report pages are planned/spec'd: Production Overview (statewide trend, peak years, YoY change), Well Performance (fleet decline scatter, operator bars), Well Detail (drill-through with production curve and decline projection), Operator Comparison (formation-broken cumulative BOE), Forecast & Economics (WTI break-even).

~135 DAX measures across 14 measure files and a Python ETL (pandas) feeding a SQLite + Parquet backing store organized in raw / bronze / silver / cleaned layers — the layer separation makes recompute and audit cheap.
Row-Level Security design for four roles is specified, but model enforcement still needs validation once the .pbix exists.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Specifies six business questions about Bakken production, from 'how has output trended since 1953?' down to per-well decline analysis and break-even WTI pricing for a typical well. Operator comparison + RLS design round it out so the same report can eventually be shown to multiple operators with each only seeing their own wells.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Python preps the data: pulls/cleans North Dakota production history into Parquet + a SQLite source.db across raw/bronze/silver/cleaned layers. The intended Power BI model imports cleaned data, wraps it in a star-ish model, and uses Power Query for loading transforms. The DAX layer (~135 measures across 14 files) is where the analytics work lives — YTD totals, decline rates, half-life, cumulative shares, etc.

sections.label: 03 — WHY ETL + DASHBOARD
sections.body: I could have done this with Power Query alone. Splitting the prep into a Python ETL means the cleaning logic is testable in isolation, the intermediate Parquet artifacts are inspectable, and rebuilding the model after a schema change is a script, not a click trail. It also keeps the Power Query layer lean — Power BI does presentation and DAX, the heavy lifting is upstream.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build, 15 development stages, currently in progress. The repo contains ETL structure, measure files, and design/spec notes; .pbix, screenshots, service publish, and final demo video are pending. WCAG 2.1 AA targets are documented, but final accessibility validation depends on the finished report.

projects.name: PRE-INTERVIEW AUDIO REVIEW
projects.summary: An audio review tool for hiring: upload candidate interview audio, process asynchronously through Whisper + an LLM extractor, and review transcript + summary + extracted signals in a searchable dashboard.
details: Async pipeline with explicit state machine — uploaded → transcribing → summarizing → completed/failed — start/end timestamps + error messages stored on every transition. Celery retries up to 3 times.

Reviewer dashboard with search across candidate, transcript, summary, and job title, plus filters on processing status and reviewer status. Detail page surfaces transcript, summary, extracted signals (JSON), reviewer notes, and a retry-failed-processing action.
Whisper for STT + an LLM for extraction — both have a local mock fallback so the pipeline runs end-to-end without an API key during dev.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Reviewer uploads .mp3/.wav/.m4a; Celery picks it up, runs Whisper for transcription, then an LLM extracts a summary + structured 'interview signals' (JSON). The dashboard lets the reviewer search/filter submissions, drill into one, leave notes, mark a reviewer-status, or retry a failed job.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Standard Django + DRF backend with Celery worker queue backed by Redis; SQLite by default (Postgres-ready via env). Frontend is React + TS + Vite with React Router and Axios talking to /api/submissions/. The pipeline state is stored on the model, not just in Celery — so the dashboard can show real-time progress and the retry endpoint actually has something to act on.

sections.label: 03 — DESIGN CHOICE: STATE ON THE MODEL
sections.body: It's tempting to let Celery own pipeline progress and just check task status. But that means the dashboard's 'show me what's transcribing' view requires the broker to be queryable, retry semantics get fuzzy, and history disappears as tasks expire. Persisting the FSM transitions on the Submission model gives the UI a single source of truth and makes the retry action trivial.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: MVP. End-to-end working with Whisper + an LLM (mock fallback when no key). Known gaps: file-type restriction is frontend-only — the API's FileField accepts any upload; retry endpoint resets status + clears the error message but doesn't clear prior outputs/timestamps or gate to failed-only. Not deployed; built as a hiring-workflow portfolio piece.

projects.name: WEB BUILDER LLM
projects.summary: A small Java web app that generates or modifies websites from a prompt via Gemini. The LLM returns a JSON file manifest; the app writes those files to disk and previews them in-browser.
details: Two workflows: 'New Website' from scratch and 'Modify Existing' — load a folder, send a follow-up request, get back an updated file manifest, write it to the same folder.

JSON-shaped LLM contract: response is {title, summary, features[], files[{path, content}]} plus optional imageRequests + binary/base64/mimeType for image assets. Backend supplies sensible defaults when fields are missing — lenient by design, not a strict schema reject.

New-Website flow falls back to a local starter template when GEMINI_API_KEY isn't set or Gemini fails. Modify-Existing flow falls back to the loaded project instead. Includes scripts/check-gemini-key.sh for key validation and scripts/materialize-site.mjs to write a saved JSON response to disk by hand.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Type 'a one-page portfolio for a wedding photographer in cool tones' and the app calls Gemini, gets back a structured JSON manifest of files (index.html, styles.css, optional script.js, image assets if the model supports them), writes them to a folder, and renders the result in a browser preview. The 'Modify Existing' flow takes the same approach but ships the current file contents as context so the LLM is editing instead of regenerating.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Spring Boot serves the UI and the generation API; Gemini is called over plain HTTP via java.net.http rather than an SDK so the dependency surface stays small and the prompt/response shape is auditable. The expected response is a JSON shape (title/summary/features/files, plus optional imageRequests + binary fields), with backend-supplied defaults when fields are missing. Image generation depends on whether the picked Gemini model supports image output — the dropdown surfaces that on a per-model basis.

sections.label: 03 — WHY THE STRICT JSON CONTRACT
sections.body: Most LLM-as-codegen tools dump prose-with-code-blocks and hope the parser holds up. Asking Gemini for a {files: [{path, content}]} shape moves the variance into the LLM's job and lets the backend just iterate the array. The contract is lenient (defaults supplied) rather than strict-reject — the trade-off is more recovery from imperfect responses at the cost of needing to spot-check outputs. The starter-template fallback for the New flow means the UI is never broken just because the API is.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Solo build. Tested in Chrome only. No deploy — runs locally; the generated-sites/ folder collects sample outputs in the repo.

projects.name: AI-POWERED NOTE-TAKING APP
projects.summary: A full-stack AI notes monorepo with rich-text editing, auth, note/folder/tag organization, semantic search, summarization, OCR upload plumbing, local AWS emulation, and a hardened dev stack.
details: Turborepo workspace with web, api, ai, lambda-ocr, shared contracts, and UI packages.
Auth uses JWT access tokens, httpOnly refresh cookies, CSRF protection, Argon2 password hashes, and refresh-session rotation.
Notes use Tiptap/ProseMirror JSON with autosave, optimistic updates, version history, conflict handling, semantic search, and AI summary flows.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: The app is a production-shaped notes system: sign up, create folders/tags/notes, write rich text in a Tiptap editor, autosave drafts, inspect version history, summarize notes, and search semantically. The repo also includes OCR upload infrastructure and a local AWS-style development environment.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The stack is split into a pnpm/Turborepo monorepo: React/Vite web client, Express/TypeScript API, Python AI service, Python OCR Lambda, shared Zod/OpenAPI contracts, and a UI package. Local development runs MongoDB, Qdrant, LocalStack, the API, the AI service, and the web app through Docker Compose.

sections.label: 03 — ENGINEERING FOCUS
sections.body: The interesting part is not just the editor. The project emphasizes contracts and failure behavior: OpenAPI-generated client types, Zod validation shared across layers, autosave conflict resolution, request metrics, payload limits, Sentry hooks, and local cloud resources provisioned through Terraform.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. Phases 0-9 are documented locally, with real-AWS deployment deferred. The project is suitable for portfolio listing because the architecture, stack, and shipped phases are documented in the repo.

projects.name: ENTERPRISE TICKET MANAGEMENT SYSTEM
projects.summary: A role-based enterprise service-ticket platform for creating, assigning, tracking, commenting on, and auditing support tickets across requester, agent, manager, and admin workflows.
details: Spring Boot backend with JWT auth, role permissions, PostgreSQL persistence, Flyway migrations, Swagger/OpenAPI, and Testcontainers-ready tests.
React + TypeScript + Vite frontend with Material UI, React Router, and Axios.
Demo workflow covers requester creation, manager assignment, agent resolution, manager closure, audit timeline, and unauthorized access checks.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: A requester creates support tickets, managers assign and prioritize them, agents update assigned work and add comments, and admins manage users. The app records an audit timeline so workflow changes are inspectable.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The backend is Java 21 with Spring Boot 3, Spring Security, JWT, Spring Data JPA, Flyway, PostgreSQL, and Swagger. The frontend is React/TypeScript/Vite with Material UI. Docker Compose starts the full stack with seeded local users for each role.

sections.label: 03 — WHY IT MATTERS
sections.body: The portfolio value is the role model and lifecycle discipline: a real internal tool needs different visibility and update permissions for admins, managers, agents, and requesters. The demo workflow exercises those boundaries instead of only showing CRUD screens.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. The README has enough concrete setup, stack, seeded users, permissions, and limitations to support a portfolio entry. Email notifications and file attachments are intentionally out of scope for v1.

projects.name: MIPS SYSTEMS DIAGNOSTICS LAB
projects.summary: A low-level diagnostics project implementing MIPS assembly routines and validating them with a deterministic pass/fail harness in MARS 4.5.
details: Implements bit swapping, triple-range arithmetic, 3x3 determinant, min/max scan, XOR checksum, and null-terminated string length routines.
A Python/Bash test runner concatenates routines with tests, runs MARS in command-line mode, and validates exact pass/fail output.
Documentation covers calling convention, register mapping, debug notes, test plan, and sample output.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: The project implements small MIPS assembly routines and verifies them with a deterministic harness. The intended signal is low-level debugging discipline: registers, memory layout, bitwise operations, function contracts, and predictable tests.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: Each routine lives in its own assembly file. A build script creates one combined test assembly file, runs it through MARS 4.5, and checks that stdout reports the exact expected summary. The harness includes edge cases for bit positions, duplicate values, empty arrays, and strings.

sections.label: 03 — BOUNDARIES
sections.body: The README is explicit about scope: MARS 4.5 only, no SPIM/QtSPIM support, no floating point, no dynamic allocation, and no overflow detection. That clarity makes it easier to evaluate the work without overclaiming embedded-platform experience.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress from the unfinished folder. The repo has enough implementation notes, runnable test instructions, and expected output to justify listing it.

projects.name: MATRIXOPS CLI
projects.summary: A C++20 command-line matrix utility for CSV validation, add/subtract/multiply/transpose operations, 2x2 and 3x3 determinants, output writing, and exact-result comparison.
details: CLI supports add, subtract, multiply, transpose, determinant, validate, compare, and help commands.
CMake build with CTest unit tests plus Python functional tests against generated outputs.
Known limits are documented: simple numeric CSV only, no quoted fields, determinants limited to 2x2/3x3, and exact double comparison.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: MatrixOps loads numeric CSV matrices and performs basic operations from the command line. It can write results to CSV, validate input files, compute 2x2/3x3 determinants, and compare actual output against expected output.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The project uses standard-library C++20 with CMake. Unit tests run through CTest, while a Python functional runner exercises the built binary against end-to-end CLI cases and expected files.

sections.label: 03 — TESTING FOCUS
sections.body: The project is intentionally a small systems-testing tool. The README emphasizes command behavior, input validation, generated output, and repeatable local test commands rather than building a large math library.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress from the unfinished folder. The README has enough detail for a portfolio entry, including features, tooling baseline, usage examples, tests, and limitations.

projects.name: POCKETLM
projects.summary: An iOS on-device LLM architecture demo that wraps llama.cpp with a custom React Native TurboModule bridge and streams tokens from a C++ core through native code into JavaScript.
details: React Native / Expo app with chat, bench, and model-management surfaces planned around a custom native bridge.
Platform-neutral C++ core exposes a public C API and links to a pinned llama.cpp submodule.
Build docs describe streaming event flow, deterministic inference checks, memory lifecycle testing, and privacy boundaries.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: PocketLM demonstrates the architecture for running a local LLM inside a React Native iOS app. The goal is the bridge and lifecycle work: model loading, generation requests, token streaming, cancellation, metrics, and native crash visibility.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The app layer is React Native 0.83 with Expo SDK 55. A TurboModule interface calls into Swift/Obj-C++ bridge code, which forwards into a C++ core linked with llama.cpp. Tokens stream back through an event emitter to JavaScript.

sections.label: 03 — DESIGN CHOICE: CUSTOM BRIDGE
sections.body: The build document rejects pre-made React Native llama wrappers because the bridge is the point. Writing the TurboModule and C++ boundary directly is what demonstrates native-module design, event ordering, cancellation, and memory lifecycle control.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. There is no top-level README yet, but the local build document contains enough concrete scope, architecture, repository layout, stack, and success criteria to list it responsibly.

projects.name: SAGUNA CONSULTING AI SYSTEM
projects.summary: A capstone system for ranking funded startups, managing outreach, and generating AI-assisted sales messages from YC, TechCrunch, enrichment, and scoring pipelines.
details: FastAPI backend handles startups, Gmail, outreach, AI services, Supabase access, and Vercel API entrypoint compatibility.
Vite frontend provides the multi-page operator UI.
ETL pipelines ingest YC and TechCrunch data, enrich companies, score ideal customer profile fit, and write runtime outputs under ignored data paths.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: The system helps a consulting workflow find funded startups, score them against an ideal customer profile, manage outreach, integrate Gmail, and generate AI-assisted sales messages.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The canonical FastAPI app lives in backend.index:app, with compatibility shims for Vercel. The frontend is a Vite multi-page React app. ETL modules ingest YC and TechCrunch sources, run enrichment/scoring, and persist schema-backed data through Supabase.

sections.label: 03 — ICP MODEL
sections.body: The README documents scoring criteria: high-fit startups have at least $250k in total funding, are not launched or still in progress, and were founded within the last year. Medium and low tiers relax those thresholds and product-stage signals.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. This is the canonical full-stack Saguna repo; the older Saguna_Consult folder is listed separately as a prototype/predecessor so the portfolio distinguishes the two.

projects.name: SAGUNA CONSULT PIPELINE PROTOTYPE
projects.summary: An earlier Saguna prototype made of focused pipeline modules for startup ingestion, normalization, LLM-ready payloads, contact enrichment, Gmail outreach, and frontend-serving database refreshes.
details: Pipeline modules ingest YC and TechCrunch data, normalize companies, prepare LLM payloads, enrich contacts, and materialize app-facing tables.
Outreach modules include Gmail draft/send flows, a Gmail-style inbox prototype, and Microsoft email API experiments.
A standalone React/HeroUI rebuild explores dashboard UI while keeping backend and pipeline integration separate.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: This folder is the earlier modular version of the Saguna work. It focuses on startup data ingestion, company normalization, LLM-ready payload construction, contact enrichment, outreach workflows, and frontend-facing database materialization.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The prototype is split into small Python modules: YC and TechCrunch ingestion, unified company normalization, LLM payload building, deterministic contact extraction, app table refresh, Gmail outreach, and Microsoft email experiments. The rebuild folder contains a standalone React/HeroUI dashboard rebuild.

sections.label: 03 — MODULE SHAPE
sections.body: The repo documents each stage independently: contact enrichment explains source pages and extracted candidates, unified normalization records source provenance, LLM-ready payloads preserve field-level evidence, and the app refresh step materializes lead/ranking/dashboard tables for the frontend.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. This is listed as a prototype/predecessor because it has enough local documentation and code to describe, while the separate Saguna Consulting AI System entry represents the more canonical full-stack version.

projects.name: SENTIMENT ANALYZER DASHBOARD
projects.summary: A React dashboard that sends text to a Python AWS Lambda container and returns sentiment, emotion probabilities, and keyword extraction.
details: Frontend is React 19 + TypeScript + Vite with planned GitHub Pages deployment.
Backend Lambda container runs Cardiff RoBERTa sentiment, j-hartmann DistilRoBERTa emotion, and YAKE keywords.
CI/CD uses GitHub Actions with OIDC-based AWS authentication and a Lambda Function URL for the HTTPS API.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Users enter text in a dashboard and receive sentiment classification, emotion breakdown, and extracted keywords. The frontend stores history client-side, while inference runs behind an HTTPS Lambda Function URL.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The backend is a Python 3.13 Lambda container with transformer models baked into the image and YAKE for statistical keyword extraction. The frontend is a Vite React app that posts to the function URL; production builds inline the deployed Lambda URL for GitHub Pages.

sections.label: 03 — DEPLOYMENT CHOICE
sections.body: The backend uses a Lambda Function URL instead of API Gateway to keep the deployment small and direct. The README documents the CORS constraint: local preview can smoke asset inlining, but real CORS validation has to happen from the deployed GitHub Pages origin.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. Backend Lambda is deployed and Phase 2 is complete; frontend work is the current next step. The README has enough stack, architecture, model, setup, and roadmap detail for a portfolio entry.

projects.name: TASK MANAGEMENT SYSTEM (.NET)
projects.summary: A full-stack ASP.NET Core task manager with JWT auth, user-scoped tasks/categories, filtering, pagination, soft deletes, activity logs, validation, Swagger, and application-layer tests.
details: Solution is split into Domain, Application, Infrastructure, Api, and Tests projects.
Backend uses EF Core migrations, repository interfaces, a Result pattern, global exception middleware, JWT token generation, and FluentValidation.
Static SPA is served from the API host and supports auth, categories, task CRUD, filters, pagination, and soft delete.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: Users register or log in, manage categories, create and update tasks, filter by status/priority/search, page through task lists, and soft-delete work items. Activity logs track task status changes.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The backend is an ASP.NET Core REST API with EF Core and SQLite. The application layer returns Result objects instead of throwing for business failures; FluentValidation handles request DTOs; global middleware maps unexpected errors to clean JSON.

sections.label: 03 — ARCHITECTURE SIGNAL
sections.body: The project is useful as a clean architecture exercise: domain entities stay thin, the application layer owns use cases and Result responses, infrastructure owns persistence and security helpers, and the API layer owns controllers, validators, middleware, and Swagger.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. Core functionality is implemented but the README notes that broader validation and end-to-end testing still need refinement.

projects.name: CAREMATCH AI
projects.summary: A mobile-first healthcare routing prototype that turns free-text symptoms into editable clinical summaries, safety-aware guidance, provider matches, and a booking flow.
details: Frontend collects symptom text, shows a staged AI-summary flow, renders editable clinical cards, and walks through provider matching and booking.
Express backend exposes health and symptom-summary endpoints with strict schema validation and multi-provider LLM adapters.
Safety layer avoids diagnosis language, flags urgent terms, pauses matching for urgent guidance, and falls back to deterministic mock data when providers fail.

sections.label: 01 — WHAT IT DOES
sections.body: A patient describes symptoms in plain language. The app organizes the intake into review cards, asks a safety-oriented clarifying question, recommends a starting care direction, shows matched providers, and walks the user through selecting an appointment slot.

sections.label: 02 — HOW IT WORKS
sections.body: The backend normalizes LLM output into a schema-validated payload. It can call OpenAI, xAI/Grok, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, Qwen, or a mock fallback. The frontend keeps the user in the loop by making generated clinical cards editable before matching.

sections.label: 03 — SAFETY BOUNDARY
sections.body: The project is careful not to present itself as a diagnosis tool. It uses clinician-review language, keeps summaries editable, flags urgent safety terms, and pauses provider matching when emergency guidance is more appropriate.

sections.label: 04 — STATUS
sections.body: Proceeding / in progress. The README is detailed enough for portfolio use and clearly lists current limitations: mock provider data, simulated booking, simulated insurance/location/availability matching, and no production compliance work yet.
